nodes.goal: Replace the old third-party CRM mental model with a Payload-native CRM workspace, Stripe webhook billing sync, and Payload-jobs-driven Resend sequences that power compact admin and customer account surfaces.

nodes.label: AI ops assistant (beta)
nodes.goal: Optional, feature-flagged staff employee copilot: portfolio-style Assistant UI and Tool UI patterns, internal-docs RAG using the same efficient local-ingestion mindset as the portfolio and Magicborn flow, and assistant-rendered ops components for tours, assigned tasks, and follow-up contacts. nodes.summary: Use Payload CMS (with-vercel-website template) on Vercel with PostgreSQL hosted on Supabase. Swap template DB env from Neon to Supabase connection string when implementing.

nodes.label: Where internal process documentation lives
nodes.summary: Repo may stay public for transparency if we avoid secrets and customer PII in commits. High-level `.planning/` is OK; detailed SOPs can move to private repo, Notion, or Payload admin-only collections. Customers only see public pages and appropriate forms, not internal quotes or ops tools.

nodes.label: Payload-native CRM replaces third-party CRM providers
nodes.summary: Grime Time will own leads, contacts, accounts, opportunities, activities, and follow-up state inside Payload instead of depending on HubSpot or EngageBay.

nodes.label: Marketing site content is seed-first, with quick-edit proof in Payload admin
nodes.summary: The launch site should come from repeatable seed content, then allow staff to update reviews, testimonials, before/after proof, and public customer-support pages quickly in Payload admin without touching code.

nodes.label: Marketing imagery should stay Payload-driven, not trapped in homepage-only React content
nodes.summary: Hero art, service cards, supporting proof, and background imagery should be seeded into Payload page layout blocks and media records wherever practical. Avoid leaving core public messaging inside home-only hardcoded React panels that editors cannot reshape through the normal page model.

nodes.label: Ops dashboard is Payload-first, not external-CRM-gated
nodes.summary: `/ops` should operate from Payload business records, scheduled work, scorecards, and later an internal follow-up queue. External CRM reads are no longer part of the intended operating model.

nodes.label: Payload quotes stay the source of truth and feed an internal pipeline
nodes.summary: Quote detail, pricing logic, tax notes, and lifecycle state live in Payload. Any pipeline or follow-up model should derive from internal Payload opportunity records, not mirrored deals in a third-party CRM.

nodes.label: Lifecycle automation uses Payload jobs and Resend
nodes.summary: Follow-up sequences, reminders, and lifecycle emails should be orchestrated through Payload background jobs and delivered through Resend rather than a CRM automation suite.

nodes.label: Mirror HubSpot CRM primitives in a first-party Payload model
nodes.summary: The in-house CRM should copy the useful shape of HubSpot, not its vendor dependency: records, properties, associations, owners, pipelines, activities, tasks, search, and sequences all become first-party collections, globals, API routes, and jobs.

nodes.label: Accounts represent both households and companies, with commercial depth built in
nodes.summary: The `accounts` model is the shared first-party record for residential households and commercial companies. Commercial relationships need extra billing, AP, tax-exemption, and multi-location fields instead of being treated like a thin variant of a homeowner record.

nodes.label: CRM workspace UX favors compact tabbed layouts over overlapping panels
nodes.summary: The first-party CRM should avoid overlapping sheets and accidental overflow. Prefer tabs, tab groups, button groups, inline detail panes, badges, info icons, and tooltips inside stable layout regions.

nodes.label: Stripe handles payments; Payload owns billing state
nodes.summary: Stripe will process payment methods, invoices, subscriptions, checkout, and webhook events. Payload remains the business-facing source of truth for invoice context, customer-visible billing history, subscription policy, and operational status.

nodes.label: Default billing UX is Stripe-hosted, portal-first for account holders, and supports internal adjustments

nodes.label: Homepage instant quote stays fast, but optional customer photo series is part of the intake contract
nodes.summary: The public estimator on `/#instant-quote` should remain a low-friction range tool, but customers may optionally attach a small series of property or problem photos with the same request so staff can scope the job faster without forcing an upload requirement on every lead.

nodes.label: Public estimate math is calibratable guidance, not a fixed truth table
nodes.summary: The current square-footage-first estimator is useful for speed but too blunt to be treated as settled pricing. nodes.summary: Any view that exposes cross-user or cross-company information, internal docs, or operational controls is staff/admin-only. Customer and public surfaces must stay limited to public plus self/account-scoped data, with company-admin customers limited to their own company membership management.

Ship a change to the GAD framework itself (new skill, new subagent, reworked command, altered loop) in a way that preserves the ability to interpret eval results across versions. Trigger this skill whenever a framework change could affect how an agent behaves during an eval run — e.g. adding a mandatory skill, changing a command's spawned subagent, rewording AGENTS.md, changing the plan-phase flow, anything that would make "the same agent prompt" produce different output. The goal is to make framework drift visible so we don't mistake a framework improvement for an agent improvement (or vice versa) when scores move.
